description: A systematic framework for identifying, analyzing, and rewriting automatic behaviors by manipulating the cue-routine-reward cycle and leveraging environmental design.
principles: Map the complete habit loop before attempting change.
Isolate the underlying craving through reward experimentation.
Apply the golden rule: preserve cue and reward, replace routine.
Design the environment to make cues unavoidable and routines frictionless.
Anchor change in a keystone habit to trigger positive cascades.
Build belief through community accountability and small wins.
Automate responses using explicit implementation intentions.

workflow.purpose: Understand the current automatic behavior pattern.
guidelines: Record the cue, routine, and reward for at least three instances.
Use the five cue data points: location, time, emotional state, other people, preceding action.
Avoid assuming the routine itself is the reward.
pitfalls: Confusing the neurological reward with the physical routine.
Ignoring subtle emotional triggers that initiate the loop.

workflow.purpose: Identify the true neurological drive behind the behavior.
guidelines: Test different potential rewards for 15 minutes when the cue hits.
Observe which option satisfies the urge and reduces the craving.
Repeat the test to confirm the primary driver.
pitfalls: Skipping the experimentation phase and guessing the reward.
Assuming the routine delivers the reward without verification.

workflow.purpose: Replace the habit without breaking the neurological loop.
guidelines: Keep the cue and reward identical to the original loop.
Design a new routine that delivers the exact same reward.
Practice the new routine consistently until it becomes automatic.
pitfalls: Abandoning the reward entirely, which breaks the loop's reinforcement.

workflow.purpose: Make cues visible and routines frictionless.
guidelines: Place cues in high-traffic or unavoidable locations.
Remove physical or digital obstacles to the new routine.
Pre-commit to the new routine by preparing materials in advance.
pitfalls: Relying solely on willpower instead of environmental design.
Leaving old cues in the environment to trigger fallback behaviors.

workflow.purpose: Create systemic change through high-leverage behaviors.
guidelines: Select one habit that naturally improves other areas of life.
Track progress daily to build momentum and self-efficacy.
Allow positive spillover effects to reinforce broader changes.
pitfalls: Attempting to change multiple habits simultaneously.
Neglecting consistency during the initial adaptation phase.

workflow.purpose: Sustain change through social reinforcement and shared expectations.
guidelines: Share goals with a support group or accountability partner.
Celebrate small wins publicly to reinforce belief in change.
Participate in rituals that signal commitment to the new identity.
pitfalls: Isolating the change process from social networks.
Underestimating the role of shared belief in overcoming resistance.

checklist: All three loop components (cue, routine, reward) are explicitly identified.
The underlying craving has been isolated through reward experimentation.
The new routine delivers the exact same reward as the old one.
Environmental cues are positioned to trigger the new routine automatically.
A keystone habit has been selected to support broader behavioral shifts.
A support system or accountability mechanism is in place.
Implementation intentions (If-Then plans) are written for high-risk situations.

vocabulary.definition: The neurological pattern consisting of cue, routine, and reward that automates behavior.
A single behavior that, when changed, triggers widespread positive shifts in other habits.
The underlying neurological anticipation that fuels the habit loop and makes it automatic.
The concept that self-control operates like a finite resource that strengthens with repeated use.

heuristics: If the routine changes but the cue and reward stay the same, the habit persists.
Small wins compound into systemic transformation over time.
antipatterns: Relying on motivation instead of environmental design.
Changing the cue or reward while attempting to modify the routine.

examples.context: A professional struggling with afternoon slumps and unhealthy snacking.
examples.outcome: Identified stress as the cue, replaced vending machine trips with a 5-minute walk, maintained the reward of mental reset, and eliminated the craving-driven routine.

examples.context: A team aiming to improve meeting efficiency and decision speed.
examples.outcome: Adopted a standing meeting cue, kept the reward of clear decisions, changed the routine to 15-minute focused sprints, resulting in faster resolutions and reduced fatigue.
